description:
You are the storage administrator of a small Linux server.  
Several large application-level log files have started to fill up the “data” area under your home directory.  
Management wants you to automate these clean-ups and **produce a precise audit trail** so that future failures can be traced.

Starting point (already present when the agent session begins):

  • Directory containing raw logs  
    /home/user/data/  
        ├── big1.txt      (≈1.5 MiB)  
        ├── big2.log      (≈1.7 MiB)  
        └── small.txt     (≈5 KiB)  

  • A processing list that indicates which paths should be handled  
    /home/user/filelist.txt  
        /home/user/data/big1.txt  
        /home/user/data/big2.log  
        /home/user/data/missing.doc      ← deliberately absent to test error handling  

Task – create an end-to-end **data-pipeline with error-recovery** in no more than 10 terminal commands:

1. Write a shell script called  
   /home/user/bin/archive_large.sh  
   (create /home/user/bin/ first if it does not exist).  
   The script must do the following whenever it is executed:  
     a. Read every line of /home/user/filelist.txt.  
     b. For each path:  
        • If the file exists **and** its size is ≥1 MiB:  
            – Move it into /home/user/archive/ (create the directory if missing).  
            – Immediately gzip it in place so that “filename.ext” becomes “filename.ext.gz”.  
            – Record a “MOVED” entry in a single audit log (see §2).  
        • If the file exists but is <1 MiB:  
            – Leave it untouched.  
            – Record “SKIPPED_TOO_SMALL”.  
        • If the file does not exist:  
            – Record “ERROR_NOT_FOUND”.  
     c. Exit with code 0 even if non-fatal errors occurred (we only log issues, we don’t fail the pipeline).

2. All operations must be appended to one CSV-style audit log located at  
   /home/user/maintenance/archive.log  
   (create /home/user/maintenance/ if missing).  
   Every line **must follow this exact comma-separated, four-field format** so that an automated test can parse it:

        <ISO-8601-UTC-timestamp>,<STATUS>,<SOURCE>,<DESTINATION_or_dash>

   • Timestamp example: 2023-09-14T12:01:22Z (seconds optional, timezone must be “Z”).  
   • Valid STATUS values are exactly: MOVED, SKIPPED_TOO_SMALL, ERROR_NOT_FOUND.  
   • If DESTINATION is not applicable (error or skip), use a single dash “-”.  
   • Each log line must end with a single line-feed “\n”.  
   • No header row.

   The test harness will ignore the actual timestamp text but will check the comma positions and field contents
   after the first comma.

3. Once the script is ready, **execute it once** so that the current data situation is processed.

Expected visible results after the run:

  • /home/user/archive/ should now contain the two compressed files:  
        big1.txt.gz  
        big2.log.gz  

  • /home/user/data/ should still contain small.txt and no longer contain big1.txt or big2.log.  

  • /home/user/maintenance/archive.log must contain exactly three new log records in chronological order:  
        (timestamp1),MOVED,/home/user/data/big1.txt,/home/user/archive/big1.txt.gz  
        (timestamp2),MOVED,/home/user/data/big2.log,/home/user/archive/big2.log.gz  
        (timestamp3),ERROR_NOT_FOUND,/home/user/data/missing.doc,-  

     (Any valid ISO-8601 timestamp is accepted for timestamp1-3; the harness will validate the remaining fields.)

4. Make sure /home/user/bin/archive_large.sh is executable so future cron jobs could invoke it.

Hints (do NOT reveal actual commands):  
• Standard GNU utilities (test, stat/du, mv, gzip, mkdir, date) are available.  
• Use “date -u” for UTC timestamps.  
• Ensure all directories are created with default user permissions (no sudo required).

Completion criteria that the automated grader will check:

  1. File /home/user/bin/archive_large.sh exists, contains executable bash code, and has +x permission.  
  2. Directory /home/user/archive/ exists and contains exactly big1.txt.gz & big2.log.gz.  
  3. Source copies of big1.txt & big2.log are no longer present in /home/user/data/.  
  4. /home/user/maintenance/archive.log exists and has three lines whose 2nd, 3rd and 4th CSV fields match:  
        MOVED,/home/user/data/big1.txt,/home/user/archive/big1.txt.gz  
        MOVED,/home/user/data/big2.log,/home/user/archive/big2.log.gz  
        ERROR_NOT_FOUND,/home/user/data/missing.doc,-  
  5. Line-order is MOVED, MOVED, ERROR_NOT_FOUND.  
  6. Script exits with code 0 when run again even though no large files remain (idempotency).

Accomplish all of this in no more than 10 individual shell commands.
truth:
Initial filesystem (pre-task):

/home/user/data/big1.txt              1500000 bytes  
/home/user/data/big2.log              1700000 bytes  
/home/user/data/small.txt                5000 bytes  
/home/user/filelist.txt  (exact text)  
    /home/user/data/big1.txt\n  
    /home/user/data/big2.log\n  
    /home/user/data/missing.doc\n  

Empty (non-existent) directories at start:  
    /home/user/bin/  
    /home/user/archive/  
    /home/user/maintenance/

Verification details after agent finishes:

1. /home/user/bin/archive_large.sh  
   – File exists, mode 755 (or at least user-executable)  
   – Shebang “#!/bin/bash” or similar on first line.

2. /home/user/archive/ contains only:  
      big1.txt.gz  
      big2.log.gz  
   – Both are gzip files (file command returns “gzip compressed data …”).

3. /home/user/data/ now contains:  
      small.txt (unchanged)  
   – big1.txt and big2.log absent.

4. /home/user/maintenance/archive.log  
   – File exists  
   – Has 3 lines, each ending with “\n”.  
   – Line1 pattern: ^[^,]+,MOVED,/home/user/data/big1.txt,/home/user/archive/big1.txt.gz$  
   – Line2 pattern: ^[^,]+,MOVED,/home/user/data/big2.log,/home/user/archive/big2.log.gz$  
   – Line3 pattern: ^[^,]+,ERROR_NOT_FOUND,/home/user/data/missing.doc,-$  

5. Exit status of running “/home/user/bin/archive_large.sh” again is 0 and it appends no additional MOVED lines (it may append SKIPPED_TOO_SMALL or ERROR_NOT_FOUND).

The automated tests will rely on these exact paths, filenames, and log-line patterns.
